observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to: 

Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all her collections daily or not later than the next banking day; 



Require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare a reconciliation of the amount of weekly/monthly collection vis-à-vis the amount of weekly/monthly deposit as recorded in the Cashbook by submitting the breakdown of the collections represented by each deposit slip; and

Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Revenue Clerks that the official receipts be written in hard indelible ballpen and the duplicate and triplicate copies will be clear carbon reproductions in all respects of whatever may have been written on the original.
observations.managementAction:
Deposit intact all collections at least once a week





Prepare a reconciliation of the amount of weekly/monthly collection vis-à-vis the amount of weekly/monthly deposit as recorded in the Cashbook




Official Receipts will be written in hard indelible ballpen and the duplicate and triplicate copies will be clear carbon reproductions in all respects of whatever may have been written on the original
observations.reason:
Depositing the collections daily is not attainable / unreasonable due to its bank locations. Thus, making us decide to deposit at least once a week.

There is difficulty in preparing a breakdown of the collections for the year 2023 as represented by each deposit slip since there is no proper turnover of documents from the previous Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:

1. Municipal Accountant to:

a. Record donations in cash under the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account in the Trust Fund-DRRM books and ensure that the donations are duly supported by the Report of Collection and Deposit (RCD) and supporting documents submitted by the Municipal Treasurer, or based on the credit memo if the donations are directly deposited by the donor to the agency bank account; 

b. Furnish the Budget Unit copies of the RCD, Journal Entry Voucher and credit memo as bases in the preparation of the Registry of Cash Donations and Utilization (RCDU);

c. Coordinate with Municipal Budget Officer on the preparation of the Report on the Receipts and Utilization of DRRMF sourced from GAA and Cash Donations; and

d. Require the CDUR, duly signed by the head of the requesting unit and the budget officer, as attachment to the disbursement voucher on the utilization of cash donations.

2. Municipal Treasurer to:

a. Ensure the received donations in cash should be deposited in the bank account exclusively maintained for the DRRM fund; and

b. Submit to the Accounting Office a separate RCD for DRRM and other supporting documents such as Official Receipts and deposit slips pertaining to the donations in cash received, for recording in the books of accounts of the Municipality.

3. Municipal Budget Officer to:

a. Prepare RCDU per donor and per purpose, and timely update the registry by posting the transactions based on the source documents such as the RCD for the receipt and the CDUR for its utilization; and

b. Coordinate with the Accounting Unit on the preparation of the Report on the Receipt and Utilization of DRRMF sourced from GAA and Cash Donations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to Management that the:
Property Custodian prepare separate property cards on all classes of PPE as prescribed in the Manual on NGAS for LGUs;
Municipal Accountant maintain separate ledger cards of all PPE accounts; and
Municipality’s Inventory Committee prepare the RPCPPE after the conduct of the physical inventory count and submit the same to the Audit Team for evaluation not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and/or the Municipal Accountant:
Assign a designated personnel to trace the differences between the cashbook and accounting records for all funds in order to resolve this recurring audit observation;
Conduct monthly reconciliation of the records in the cash books and accounting records; and
Fast-track the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statements to substantiate the cash in bank balance reported in the year-end financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide depreciation expenses to all depreciable PPE accounts for the current and prior years, duly supported with lapsing schedules for the computation of depreciation expenses.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the following adjusting entry to correct the erroneous recording of the transactions mentioned above and to ensure that transactions are recorded in the correct year it occurred or consummated:
Prior period Adjustments		   ₱68,740.00
CIP – Infrastructure Assets/
Water Supply Systems			2,634,475.00
Government Equity				₱2,703,215.00

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Provide the necessary assistance to the Accounting Office to fast-track the preparation of the subsidiary ledgers manually, pending the implementation of an accounting system; and
Hasten the implementation of an accounting system that will institutionalize the record keeping and financial reporting of the LGU that can comply with the reportorial requirements of COA and the different oversight agencies.

Monitor closely the preparation of the necessary Schedules of Remittance and prepare the corresponding Subsidiary Ledgers so that withheld amounts could be remitted in full and within the prescribed deadlines; and
Assign a personnel to reconcile and validate the remaining unremitted balances to BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit explanation/justification on the noted findings. We further recommended and Management agreed to adhere to the provisions of EO No. 77 specifically the criteria for authorized official travel.

We recommended that Management:
Expedite the implementation of the abovementioned project and monitor implementation to ensure effective and efficient project implementation and reporting; and
Ensure that the development projects funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit written justification why further authorization from the SB were not secured in the current year’s PPAs in the implementation of programs and projects under lump-sum appropriations in the 20% DF despite the issuance of the same observation in the previous year; and
Secure authorization from the Sangguniang Bayan for the above-mentioned projects implemented under lump-sum appropriation and submit to the Audit Team for verification or a written explanation why the same could not be submitted.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the MDRRM Officer and the Municipal Budget Officer to determine the appropriations for CO in the LDRRMF and exclude it when transferring the unexpended balance of the current year’s appropriation for LDRRMF to the STF account; and
Through the MDRRM Officer, submit proof that the abovementioned transactions charged against the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF in the Trust Fund were included in the LDRRMFIP, otherwise submit justification of its implementation despite its non-inclusion in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that Management:
Develop and maintain a GAD Database containing gender statistics and age- and sex-disaggregated data. This database should be regularly updated to serve as a basis for gender-responsive planning, programming, and policy formulation;
Issue an Executive or Administrative Order to create a GAD M&E Team. This team should be responsible for monitoring the implementation of GAD PPAs and evaluating their outcomes to ensure the efficient and effective execution of gender-responsive initiatives; and
Direct the responsible officials to submit the DILG-endorsed GPB together with its endorsement, and the GAD Accomplishment Report for CY 2024, duly supported by the following: (1) brief summary of the reported program or project; (2) copies of reported policy issuances; (3) results of HGDG tests, if any; and (4) actions taken by the agency on the COA audit findings and recommendations, if any.

We recommended and Management, through the Local Chief Executive and other concerned officials, agreed to submit justification/explanation as to the noted deficiency and that the Municipal Budget Officer also agreed to ensure that appropriations for the PS Budget only includes those that are PS items as enumerated in the DBM LBC No. 145.

We recommended that Management:
Submit written justification on the significant delay of the implementation of the YPHP and provide proof that the calibrated actions in response to delays in the implementation of infrastructure projects provided in GPPB Circular 03-2019 dated March 8, 2019 were adopted;
Expedite the termination of the contract with the contractor and its eventual blacklisting and take over the implementation of the project or award it to another qualified contractor; and
Exhaust all legal remedies for the recovery of the balance of the mobilization fee paid to the contractor and forfeit the performance security/bond to defray the expenses to be incurred to continue the project.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit the pending AAPSI for CY 2023;
Ensure future submissions are made within sixty (60) days from receipt of the AAR; and
Review and strengthen internal processes for monitoring and reporting corrective actions to ensure that all relevant documentation is accurately compiled and submitted as required.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to hasten the reconciliation of these PPE and Breeding Stocks accounts under the Trust Fund and initiate the transfer to the General Fund by preparing the necessary journal entries to address this recurring audit observation.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and Management agreed to the following:
On the unutilized/not fully implemented PPAs, Management apply effective methods and fast-track the implementation of the pending PPAs to attain the purpose in which these funds were given to the Municipality; and
On the unreturned funds after project completion and dormant/inactive funds, Management, after thorough reconciliation of the Municipal Accountant of the balances, coordinate with the source agencies and request for authority to transfer these unexpended balances to the General Fund of the Municipality. Otherwise, remit it to the National Treasury or return the unexpended balance of completed PPAs to the source agency, whichever is appropriate.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to:
Submit justification on the above-noted deficiencies;
Stop the practice of splitting purchase requisitions or purchase orders/contracts in order to promote effective competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain an equitable contract for the best interest of the government; and
Adhere to the provisions in RA No. 9184 or RA No. 12009, the New Government Procurement Act, on the conduct of competitive bidding and alternative modes of procurement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

1.	Instruct the Inventory Committee to ensure the timely submission of the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) to the Audit Team by January 31 each year, and reconcile the results of the report with the accounting records.


2.	Require the Municipal Accountant to:

a.	Derecognize from the PPE accounts the items costing less than P50,000.00 and apply the guidelines stated in COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024 regarding their reclassification to semi-expendable property, inventory, or expense, as applicable.  Henceforth, apply the P50,000.00 capitalization threshold when recording PPE;


b.	Review and reconcile the PPE accounts presented in the books and lapsing schedules, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct PPE balances in the financial statements;

c.	 Update and reconcile PPE records and provide depreciation on identified assets; and




d.	Instruct the Accounting Staff assigned to exert earnest effort in the preparation and maintenance of PPE Ledger Cards, ensuring that all records are accurate and complete.



3.	Require the Property Custodian to:

a.	Prepare and maintain property cards and reconcile with the PPE Ledger Cards periodically; and



b.	Assist the Municipal Accountant in the proper reclassification of items costing less than P50,000.00 in accordance with COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024, ensuring that these are correctly transferred to the appropriate semi-expendable property, inventory, or expense accounts.  Additionally, facilitate the replacement of Property Cards with Semi-Expendable Property Cards, where applicable, ensuring that all records accurately reflect the classification, accountability, and movement of assets.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to, henceforth, follow the required timeframe on the utilization and disbursement of a particular fund transfer for the implementation of a particular project. Otherwise, cause the return of the excess a or any unutilized amount of the fund transfer to the Bureau of Treasury after the lapse of the required timeframe. 


We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:

1.	Municipal Accountant to:

a.	Exert utmost effort to verify the accounts with abnormal debit and unreturned balances and send confirmation letters once the NGA will be identified to determine if the LGU have unsettled accounts. Also, coordinate with other concerned personnel/ Department Heads to verify fund transfer which were not duly supported with documents;  

b.	Prepare regularly the Aging of Fund Transfer on a quarterly basis; and



c.	Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the fund transfer.


2.	Municipal Treasurer to, henceforth, return to the Bureau of Treasury the excess or any unutilized amount of fund transfer after the lapse of the timeframe/period to implement a particular project, such as the project funded by LGSF-FA in CY 2023, as required by the Department of Budget and Management Local Budget Circular No. 150 dated March 8, 2023.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to exhaust all possible means and exert utmost effort to account for the beginning balance of the Due to the PhilHealth account amounting to P1,203,915.72 and make adjusting entries, if necessary.


We also recommended the Employee-in-Charge of the deduction and remittance of PhilHealth premiums to:

Reconcile with the PhilHealth the records and information of all active employees in Calendar Year 2024 to update the exact premium amounts to be withheld/recorded and remitted for the personal share and government share;


Ensure that the premiums encoded in the remittance list tallies with the amounts withheld and recorded in the books; and



c.	Henceforth, conduct regular/periodic reconciliation of the premium amounts to be withheld and remitted to avoid underpaid/overpaid premium amounts.

We recommended the Employee-in-Charge in the remittance of Pag-IBIG premium contributions and loan amortizations and the Municipal Accountant/ Accounting Staff to:

a.	Reconcile the difference between the amounts of premium contributions and loans amortizations withheld vis a vis the amounts remitted as presented in Table 21 aggregating P65,983.16;


b.	Verify and reconcile records to ensure that the amounts withheld/recorded are the same as the amounts remitted for the personal share and government share. Henceforth, conduct regular/periodic reconciliation of the premium amounts to be withheld and remitted to avoid underpaid/overpaid premium contributions and loan amortizations.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to exhaust all possible means and exert utmost effort to account for the beginning balance of the Due to the HDMF account amounting to P226,952.53 and make adjusting entries, if necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to protect the Municipality against any damage or loss of its properties and the risk of non-indemnification, in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

We also recommended the Municipal Treasurer, as the Employee-in-Charge to prepare and submit to the GSIS the Property Inventory Form GSIS listing of all the insurable properties and other assets, showing their latest appraised values/valuation, appraisal date, location, and other information not later than April 30 of each year.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource Management Officer, to:

a.	Provide justification on the Municipality’s approval to send the job order personnel to attend trainings despite the absence of duties and responsibilities in the contract of service;

b.	Submit the certification that the trainings attended and the travels undertaken by the job order personnel were necessary in the fulfillment of their obligations duly approved by the Head of Agency and/or any authorized official. This is without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Disallowance, if warranted; and

c.	Formulate guidelines, policies, standards and procedures on the hiring, retention and renewal of the contract of job order personnel in consonance with the Municipality’s Organization Structure and Staffing Pattern. 


We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to process training and travel related-transactions of the job order personnel provided these are duly supported with certification that the related travels and/or trainings were necessary in the fulfillment of their obligations.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to submit written justification on the delay of the termination of the loan agreement despite the bank’s recommendation on the termination of the said agreement that resulted in the incurrence of unnecessary interest amounting to P1,084,548.05 as of December 31, 2024 despite the non-utilization of the loan proceeds from CY 2022-2024. This is without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point System-Technical Working Group to:

a.	Monitor and evaluate identified GAD PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget to ensure that these are directly aligned with the goal of promoting gender equality and women empowerment and clearly/adequately address gender issues and concerns;
 


b.	Enhance knowledge on gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD related tools, among others through attendance with relevant GAD trainings; and



c.	Maximize the utilization of GAD funds to deliver benefits for gender equality and women empowerment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairperson of the MDRRM Council (MDRRMC), require a prior review and evaluation of all PPAs before approval, and ensure that funded projects align with eligible disaster preparedness, response, and recovery efforts as enumerated per thematic areas of DRRM, as stated in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.


We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the backhoe from the DRR Equipment account to the Construction and Heavy Equipment account, and ensure the proper recording of the necessary adjusting entries.

c. Cause the preparation and submission of a complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Revenue Clerks that the official receipts be written in hard indelible ballpen and the duplicate and triplicate copies will be clear carbon reproductions in all respects of whatever may have been written on the original.

b. Reconcile every month the Driver’s Trip Ticket with the Monthly Fuel Consumption and Summary of Monthly Report of Official Travels as mandated under COA Circular No. 77-61 to determine whether the said fuel usage is valid and is being used appropriately;

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the:

b. Municipal Accountant to recognize Accounts Payable that are consummated transactions with complete documentation to prove that these are valid liabilities.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MPDC to:

Address the issues affecting the delay on the bidding-related activities and ensure that all approved PPAs are procurement and implementation-ready and warrant the implementation of all approved PPAs for the optimal utilization of the appropriation for development projects; and

Continuously adhere to the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 to ensure that maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcome of the Municipality is achieved and for the realization of the intended purpose of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the MDRRM Officer, together with the MDRRM Council to:

 Timely implement all LDRRM related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund as well as the desired benefits; and


Stop the use of LDRRMF for non-disaster related activities and strictly follow the guidelines in the utilization of the LDRRMF as provided under Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC Chairperson to submit justification why the contractor was awarded with new contracts despite incurrence of negative slippage and the non-disclosure of the actual accomplishment vis-à-vis planned accomplishment of its on-going projects.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC to submitted written justification why award of contract was still made to the particular contractor despite having poor performance in its Ongoing contracts.

Likewise, we recommended to the BAC to thoroughly scrutinize the post-qualification of the bidding documents and to require, where necessary, documents to comprehensively undertake the post-qualification of bidders in future procurement of infrastructure projects.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:

1.	Municipal Accountant to:

a.	Record donations in cash under the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account in the Trust Fund-DRRM books and ensure that the donations are duly supported by the Report of Collection and Deposit (RCD) and supporting documents submitted by the Municipal Treasurer, or based on the credit memo if the donations are directly deposited by the donor to the agency bank account; 

b.	Furnish the Budget Unit copies of the RCD, Journal Entry Voucher and credit memo as bases in the preparation of the Registry of Cash Donations and Utilization (RCDU);

c.	Coordinate with Municipal Budget Officer on the preparation of the Report on the Receipts and Utilization of DRRMF sourced from GAA and Cash Donations; and

d.	Require the CDUR, duly signed by the head of the requesting unit and the budget officer, as attachment to the disbursement voucher on the utilization of cash donations.

2.	Municipal Treasurer to:

a.	Ensure the received donations in cash should be deposited in the bank account exclusively maintained for the DRRM fund; and

b.	Submit to the Accounting Office a separate RCD for DRRM and other supporting documents such as Official Receipts and deposit slips pertaining to the donations in cash received, for recording in the books of accounts of the Municipality.

3.	Municipal Budget Officer to:

a.	Prepare RCDU per donor and per purpose, and timely update the registry by posting the transactions based on the source documents such as the RCD for the receipt and the CDUR for its utilization; and

b.	Coordinate with the Accounting Unit on the preparation of the Report on the Receipt and Utilization of DRRMF sourced from GAA and Cash Donations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

1. Municipal Accountant and the Property Custodian to maintain supplies ledger cards and stock cards, respectively, and reconcile periodically; and

2. Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical count of Inventories and prepare RPCI with complete data for each item, as basis for reconciliation with the SLCs and SCs. Also, furnish the Audit Team a copy of the duly accomplished RPCI not later than January 31 of each year in accordance with the GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to strictly comply with the posting and submission requirements of the GPPB to provide full information and transparency to the public;
We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the recording of the share of income to the Trust Fund.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile with the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHEALTH the balances and properly disclose in the Notes to Financial. Further, he should adapt monitoring mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld.
We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of untitled lots.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to send demand letter to those employees who have not yet settled their disallowances, and strictly monitor the settlement of suspension and disallowances.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to exhaust all means to settle unliquidated advances from employees concerned.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Agriculturist, Local Disaster and Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Focal Person, and Municipal Social Work and Development Officer (MSWDO to judiciously plan proposed projects and fast-track the implementation of said programs, projects and activities funded by the BUB to provide constituents of the maximum benefits due them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to expedite the processing of documents for the titling of lands owned by the Municipality.  We further recommended that before any construction is made, land ownership thru a certificate of title is obtained.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive compel the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate withholding of salaries and claims of officers and employees who failed to settle their cash advances within the prescribed period after being sent notice and demand.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive compel the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible from them for the current and prior years

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned employee in-charge in the preparation of GSIS remittances and municipal accountant to conduct thorough reconciliation with its records with that of the GSIS.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive directs the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to identify the employees with unremitted contributions to enable the Municipal Government to settle its obligations. 


The Municipal Accountant should also regularly reconcile the amounts withheld and remitted to Pag-IBIG in order to ascertain the accuracy and reliability of the balances of Due to Pag-IBIG account in the financial statements.

The Local Chief Executive should consider creating and organizing an Internal Audit Services, in compliance with R.A. No 4177 and AO 70 to give assurance for a sound and adequate internal control system in the operations of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should require the Municipal Accountant to always assign special accounts and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers in the GF for the LGUs 5% GAD & 5% LDRRMF in order to have readily adequate and reliable information as to assets, liabilities and the corresponding object of expenditures, fund balance, and if these were in line with the guidelines.

Further, advise the accounting unit to make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balance of the Road Network account; reclassify driveways as Land Improvements and depreciate separately each component of the road networks except the road lots.

Moreover, the Engineering Unit regularly inform the accountant whether or not a particular infrastructure constitutes road networks, and if any concrete pavement sections of roads are removed to enable derecognition from the records of the accounting. Also, provide the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects. Scheduled Date of Physical Count is undertaken on the last months of the Year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to secure the deed documents as well as the property acknowledgement receipts, delivery receipts, etc. And furnish the accounting office to enable the recognition thereof in the books of accounts. Further, ask the assistance of the assessor to determine the fair market value of these equipment at the time of acquisition to be used as the basis in recording and the computation of the accumulated depreciation.
observations.managementAction: Supporting Documents were secured and provided to the GSO and Accounting Office. Supporting Documents were secured and provided to the GSO and Accounting Office. The following JEVs were prepared to record the assets in the Books of the LGU: JEV2024-12-012535 JEV2024-12-012537 JEV2024-12-012539 JEV2024-12-012540 JEV2024-12-012541

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant-designate agreed to prepare the appropriate journal entries to recognize the Equipment and CIP-Infrastructure Accounts.
observations.managementAction: Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) were already prepared to reflect the proper balances of the accounts concerned.

observations.recommendation: We recommend and management agreed to strictly monitor the utilization of funds and balances of appropriations so as to prevent incurrence of irregular disbursements. In addition, strictly adhere with the guidelines on the allocation and utilization of the DRRM Fund, and refrain from charging expenditures which are not in pursuance of the four thematic areas thereof.
observations.managementAction: A copy of the DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 9 series of 2023 was furnished to the Audit Team which was the basis for the KALAHI-CIDSS-NCDDP Barangay Projects and other expenditures charged against the 5% DRRMF. Moreover, we will continue to strictly adhere with the guidelines on the allocation and utilization.

observations.recommendation: Refrain from practicing realigning unexpended balances of the government funds by way of SB Resolutions; Ensure that all development projects to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are well-planned, and procurement and implementation -ready to prevent being cancelled and/or realigned during the calendar year.
observations.managementAction: Realignments for 20% MDF unexpended balances from prior years were realized through the enactment of Municipal Ordinance. Ensure that all development projects to be funded out of the Current Year appropriations and the implementation of projects are closely monitored.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to advise the contractor to correct the remaining defect of the project as soon as possible. Whenever the same further fails to do so, terminate the contract by default, forfeit the outstanding part of the contract in favor of the Municipality including the retention money and the performance bond. Finally, rectify the defective PCCP through remove and replace scheme, and implement the same either through by administration or another procurement.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-Municipal Engineer notified the contractor concerned of the remaining defects that were observed. The contractor readily rectified the defects through removing and replacing the defective concrete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to communicate with the head of the source agency and discuss the challenges encountered during the trial operation as well as on the other options in case the plan fails. Further, ensure that they are completely aware of the initial result of the project in the local government level. Finally, follow-up the conduct of training as promised by the source agency to enable the effecient and proper procedure of operating the same.
observations.managementAction: The Department of Agriculture - Caraga Region conducted and facilitated the Monolithic Dome Operation Management Training last May 24-26, 2023 and invited the Dazzling Lite Enterprises (Contractor) as the Resource Person. Representatives from the farmers, Local Government Unit of San Francisco and other stakeholders attended the training. However, Dazzling Lite Enterprises explained that they have no Standard Operating Procedure in the operationalization of the facility. The LGU spent One Million Pesos (Php1,000,000.00) for the three-phase connection to operationalize the facility and a trial/study was conducted. After the electric connection, representatives from the LGU together with the farmers conducted the trial which lasted for one month period during the middle of August, 2023. There was no significant effect on the vegetables, spices and fruits which were placed in the dome. The desired temperature of 2 degrees Celsius was not attained during the trial but the lowest temperature recorded was 15 degrees Celsius. The electric bill for the one month operation reached as high as Php100,000.00. The LGU cannot afford to spend high electric bill for a facility that cannot serve its intended function, thus, we informed the DA-Caraga that we cannot continue with the study/trial for lack of funds to pay for the high electric consumption. The result of the trial/study was submitted to the Department of Agriculture -Caraga and the Municipal Mayor had a meeting with the Regional Executive Director to discuss the result of the trial. For action to be taken, we were informed that the DA-Caraga will request the assistance from the University of the Philippines-Postharvest Horticulture Training and Research Center for another training on sustainable use and management of the monolithic dome storage system and in maintaining and extending shelf life for horticultural produce. The DA also proposed to conduct benchmarking activity in Bohol where a similar facility is being used as a cold storage for rice seeds. In addition, the DA proposed to utilize the dome as a storage for rice, corn and vegetable seeds with appropriate training to be requested from the Bureau of Plant Industry and University of the Philippines. For the problem on electricity, the DA proposed to the Office of the Secretary a research study on renewable technology; solar Power and Biomass Gasifier to energize the facility.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management send final demand letters to the barangays with long outstanding balances, and demand refund thereof. Further, we recommended also that the management conduct a regular ocular inspection on the projects to validate existence and whether the implementing barangays adhered to the terms of the MOA.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were already sent to the concerned BLGUs through their Treasurers and Accounting Clerks/Record keepers. There were liquidations received during CY 2022 until January 2023. The Inspection Committee for Infrastructure conducted monitoring and found out that most have already substantially implemented their projects with the exception of Barangay 2 which is yet to be implemented.
observations.reason: Some BLGUs still have not presented Fund Utilization Reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Mayor: (i) consider creating the General Services Office (GSO) which functions, among others, focused on the management of property and supply management of the LGU as provided in Item (t)(2)(x) of Rule XVIII of the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991; (ii) re-evaluate the composition of the Committee ensuring that those designated as part of such Committee does not handle significant responsibilities from their main functions or positions; and avail the one-time cleansing of PPEs as prescribed in COA Circular 2020-006.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Management was able to designate General Services personnel for the task in managing the LGU as provided in Item (t)(2)(x) of Rule XVIII of the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991; (ii) re-evaluate the composition of the Committee ensuring that those designated as part of such Committee does not handle significant responsibilities from their main functions or positions; and avail the one-time cleansing of PPEs as prescribed in COA Circular 2020-006.

observations.recommendation: We also recommended that the Inventory Committee secure complete PPE records from the Accounting Office and use the same as guide in the conduct of their actual physical count.
observations.managementAction: The records from the Accounting Office were utilized as a guide in the conduct of the actual physical count.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accountant conduct an in-depth investigation to find the root cause of abnormal/negative balances and the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the other negative/abnormal balances to their proper account. Also, establish an internal control that would help prevent, detect, and correct issues as they occur.
observations.managementAction: The negative balances were identified and the Municipal Accountant was able to prepare the necessary adjusting entries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (i) The Municipal Accountant effect the necessary adjusting entries: Prior Period Adjustments ₱5,176,747.50; and (ii) Henceforth, ensure that all prior period adjustments due to effect of changes in accounting policy and adjustments due to errors be recorded under the Prior Period Adjustment account.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant was able to prepare the necessary adjusting entries.
observations.reason: Henceforth, ensure that all prior period adjustments due to effect of changes in accounting policy and adjustments due to errors be recorded under the Prior Period Adjustment account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (i) The LDRRMF were transferred to Trust Fund; (ii) The Municipal Treasurer transfer the unspent and of the prior year to the Special Trust Fund and effect the necessary adjusting entries in the General Fund and Trust Fund books as prescribed; and (iii) Henceforth, ensure the timely transfer of the unspent balance of LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMF were transferred to Trust Fund and subsequent instances were duly observed by the Municipal Accountant.
observations.reason: Henceforth, ensure the timely transfer of the unspent balance of LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Inventory Committee to submit the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) of CY 2022. 

We also recommended and Management agreed to require the concerned Offices to maintain the Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards.  Henceforth, strictly observe the submission of RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year as prescribed under Section 124, Volume I of the MNGAS.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, provide an allowance for impairment losses based on their objective assessment on the collectability of the receivable accounts in accordance with the provisions of IPSAS 29.  

Henceforth, we further recommended that Management enforce controls so as to prevent the incurrence of dormant account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare a duly certified list showing the names of the taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year which data should come from the RPTAR or Taxpayer’s Index Cards maintained at the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, thereafter, furnish the Municipal Accountant a copy to serve as basis in recording the RPT and SET receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Assessor to conduct system cleaning to remove incorrect, duplicate or otherwise erroneous entries in the system. 
Moreover, we recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile their records at the end of the year to present a uniform balance in their respective financial reports.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit an acceptable explanation or justification on the non-imposition of liquidated damages on the delayed completion of the project. 

Also, we recommended and Management agreed to compute and recoup the overpayment, representing the un-imposed liquidated damages from the contractors or from the person/s responsible for the non-imposition thereof.

Henceforth, strictly observe the provisions of Annex “E” of the RIRR of R.A. 9184 and demand or deduct liquidated damages before payment of final billing and release of retention money to the supplier.

We recommended and Management agreed to show concrete evidence of the benefit/s or accomplishment/s derived from this cross-learning visit, e.g., draft of the MFO IRR, ordinances or resolutions, or programs related to the theme of the program.  

Further, we recommended and Management agreed to submit acceptable explanation/justification as to why they were not able to secure prior authorization from the DILG and the relevance of the attendance of these 16 personnel in the cross-learning visit to avoid disallowances in audit.

Henceforth, we recommended and Management agreed to adhere to DILG-DBM JMC No. 02 dated September 23, 2016 on any future learning visits and exercise prudence in spending scarce resources of the Municipality.

We recommended and Management agreed to transfer the cost of the completed projects booked as PPE from the TF to the GF.  

Henceforth, strictly comply Section 104, 1(i) of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I on the transfer of completed projects under the Trust Fund to the General Fund.

We recommended that the Management, through the BAC Chairperson, comply with the posting requirements of the complete bidding documents, minutes of pre-bid conferences, the NTP and the related approved contracts on the Municipality’s website and in the PhilGEPS as stipulated in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Accounting Office continue with the reconciliation of the Due to BIR balance to come up with the details of the overdue amount and ensure that these are fully remitted to the BIR. 

We further recommended that, henceforth a monthly reconciliation of remittances against amounts withheld be conducted to prevent the recurrence of unreconciled/unremitted balances.

We recommended that management direct the members of the local finance committee of the Municipality of San Francisco, Cebu, to refund honoraria they received as members of said committee for CY 2012.  

Henceforth, the giving of honoraria to the members of the local finance committee be discontinued.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to conduct a complete physical count of the PPE and reconcile the same with the records of the Accounting Office and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team within the reglementary period.  

Further we recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer maintain the required subsidiary ledgers and property cards, respectively, for each type of PPE and update the PAR every three years or every time there is a change in custodianship / user of the property.

We recommended that Management submit its legal basis on the inclusion of lot acquisition under the Build and Design Scheme for evaluation by the Audit Team and to appropriately make manifestation with the National Housing Authority on the noted deficiency in the aforementioned Terms of Reference.

We recommended that the concerned municipal official be directed to refund the amount of honoraria received to avoid disallowance in audit.

We also recommended that, henceforth, strict compliance with the provisions of Section 95 of RA 7160 and COA Circular No. 2013-003 and other pertinent laws is enjoined to avoid personal liability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to provide disclosures on the face of the SCBAA and/or in the Notes to the Financial Statements the:
 
a) changes between the original and final budget;
 
b) material discrepancies between the budget and actual amounts; and 

c) reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per financial statements, to enhance the transparency of the Municipality’s financial statements.
In view of this, we recommended that management assess and review the contract entered into by the Municipality and consider revision pursuant to the authority by the Sangguniang Bayan.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the memorandum of agreement or any document to support the payment of SSS premium of the farmers and fishermen pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee submit justification and proof that the supplier is qualified to sell fishing and agricultural supplies and equipment; and the required Post-Qualification Evaluation Report to avoid disallowance in audit.
We recommended that Management submit proof that Rescue Reconnaisance I is an accredited training organization by the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to submit written justification and legal basis for the grant of rice subsidy to all the employees of the municipality, otherwise the grant of rice subsidy will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that management strictly observed the provisions of Section 313 of RA 7160.  Likewise, the financial statements pertaining to the economic enterprise be submitted to COA within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to submit explanation on the failure to file request for relief from property accountability within the reglementary period together will all the required supporting documents necessary in the evaluation of the case.

We recommended that the members of the BAC and personnel involved in the procurement strictly observe the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to achieve transparency in the procurement process.  

Also require the submission of the proof of ownership of the firearms and the certification on the peace and order situation of the municipality to support that the issuance of the firearms and procurement of ammunition is necessary.

We recommended that the concerned Municipal Mayor direct the persons liable to settle immediately the amount disallowed in audit amounting to ₱4, 254,143.00 for the payment of cash incentives to its officers and employees including the contractual personnel.

We recommended management to submit legal basis to support the disbursement / grant of Christmas Bonus as well as the Anniversary Bonus in the total amount of ₱1, 240,000.00 to prevent disallowance in audit.

We recommended management to submit legal basis to support the disbursement / grant of Calamity Assistance to the Job Order Laborers in the total amount of ₱309,150.00 to prevent disallowance in audit.

Required management to exhaust all the legal remedies to require the San Francisco farmers and Fishermen’s Trading Association to settle the long overdue loan including the interest as provided in the memorandum of Agreement.

Cause the return of the remaining balance of the fund from the Presidential Social Fund by depositing the same to the Bureau of treasury.

Prepare the necessary entry to transfer the account to DUE FROM NGOs/POs for the funds entrusted to Non-Government Organizations/Peoples’ organization for the implementation of specific government project subject to liquidation.
In view of the above, we required management to submit explanation/justification on the payment of fuel claim even if the vehicle used is a private vehicle.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer make representation to the Provincial Treasurer to confirm the validity and existence of the amount deposited and have this amount withdrawn thereat and be deposited in the municipality’s depository bank.  

Also, refrain from using account title not prescribed in the Chart of Account in order not to give misleading information to the stakeholders.

We recommended that management direct the concerned Municipal Accountant to submit to the concerned Auditor those lacking documents of the subject post-audited transactions to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in audit.  

Henceforth, the said Municipal Accountant should always see to it that the DVs covering claims against government funds are supported with complete documents pursuant to Section 4 of P. D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that management:

Submit justification as to why the procurement of 149 cans of “Reliv” food supplement should not be disallowed in audit.

Direct the BAC to review Purchase Requests thoroughly to ascertain compliance of Section 18 of IRR of RA 9184.

Adopt public bidding as the general mode of procurement and resort to alternative method of procurement only in the highly exceptional cases provided for in the IRR of R.A. 9184.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the aforecited documents as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended the following courses of action to rectify the foregoing deficiencies:

(cid:9)The Inventory Committee be directed to segregate the unserviceable properties and equipment from those properties which are still serviceable but no longer needed by the agency and enter the same in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) pursuant to Section 59, Volume I of LGU NGAS, Manual;

The Municipal Accountant should record as debit to Other Assets (251) and credit to the appropriate PPE accounts the value of obsolete and unserviceable assets including property and equipment no longer used in the agency operation but still serviceable pursuant to Section 140 of LGU NGAS Manual, Volume 3; and

The Municipal Treasurer should apply for the disposal of said unserviceable properties for approval by the Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan and forward the duly approved IIRUP to the Auditor for his appropriate action pursuant to Section 379 of RA 7160.

We recommended management to stop immediately paying employer’s share to the Fund in excess of what is required by law and instead comply strictly with the above regulations effective immediately to avoid disallowance in audit.   

Also, coordinate with Pag-ibig on the possible application of the over remittance of employer’s (government) share for succeeding dues on monthly contribution, otherwise, the excessive remittance shall be disallowed in audit pursuant to Section 103 of P.D. 1445.

Required the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly analyze long overdue reconciling items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements of General, Special Education and Trust Fund and take up in the books those valid items through the issuance of Journal Entry Voucher duly supported by the appropriate documents. 

Further, the Municipal Accountant should request the concerned depository bank to adjust their books of the unrecorded transfer of Bank Account No. 014C-1130-021668 of ₱31, 832.00 and the overstatement in recording the checks No. 18311285 dated May 27, 2009 and Check No. 1831442 dated June 24, 2009 in the amount of ₱500.00 and ₱272.01, respectively.  

Moreover, require the concerned Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to adhere strictly to the provisions of Section 59 of LGU NGAS Manual, Volume I.  Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should make the necessary Journal Entries on the stale checks in accordance with Section 61 of same manual.

Refund the amount; and 


Refrain from disbursing honoraria not in accordance with Budget Circular No. 2004-5 dated March 23, 2004.

The Municipal officials concerned should refund immediately the disallowed honorarium amounting to ₱106, 052.50. 

Appropriate sanctions must be filed against the officials who deliberately collected the honorarium and those who authorized the collection thereof despite their knowledge of its irregularity.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant – Designate to effect appropriate adjusting entries to recognize/record in the books the 6 buildings and its related accumulated depreciation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant is in the process of gathering the supporting documents for the accumulated recognition of the depreciation.
observations.reason: No adjusting entries were made to record/recognize the 6 buildings and the accumulated depreciation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant – Designate effect the necessary adjustments for the recognition of the prior year’s depreciation and the corresponding depreciation expense for the PPE. Thereafter, ensure the provision of annual depreciation, whenever applicable. Seek assistance in the computation of the same, if necessary.
observations.managementAction: Initial identification for the recognition of depreciation for the respective PPEs were already undertaken.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant has yet to provide depreciation for the said PPEs and No adjustments for the accumulated depreciation of the same were made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enact measures to: a. Enforce the immediate liquidation of Cash Advances by concerned officials and employees by sending demand letters; noting that sanctions may be enforced should they fail to settle their advances by withholding their salary; and b. Ensure that Cash Advances be liquidated in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: Management had already set controls in place and had significantly reduced the balances of cash advances pending liquidation.
observations.reason: Although the Management had already set controls in place and had significantly reduced the balances of cash advances pending liquidation, the subject account still bears the balance of ₱235,000.00 as of Dec. 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant – Designate review all the entries in the general ledger of the Due to BIR, Due to GSIS and Due to PhilHealth accounts to determine the causes of abnormal balance of the accounts and make appropriate adjustments.
observations.managementAction: None
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II Finding No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management institutionalize the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office by appropriating and hiring the statutory/mandatory positions in-charge of administration and training, research and planning and operations and warning as required under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-SCS JMC Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 to assure timely implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and management.
observations.managementAction: Management had already initiated the hiring of personnel for the LDRRMO office.
observations.reason: Management is yet to fully institutionalize the statutory/mandatory positions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant agreed to reconcile their respective records of Cash in Local Treasury as of December 31, 2023 and submit the results of reconciliation to the Audit Team for review and to conduct the subsequent reconciliation regularly in order to timely effect the necessary corrections and/or adjustments and consistently harmonize the balances at year-end.

We recommended and Management, through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to hasten the implementation of the eNGAS accounting system, otherwise manually maintain the required subsidiary ledgers and schedules in support of the GL account balances pending its implementation.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the:
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant reconcile their respective records of Cash in Bank account as of December 31, 2023 and submit results of reconciliation to the Audit Team for review and effect the necessary corrections and/or adjustments to present an accurate cash in bank balances at year-end; and
Municipal Accountant fast-track the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statements to substantiate the cash in bank balance reported in the year-end financial statements.

We recommended and Management, through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to reconcile the subject reciprocal accounts and prepare the necessary adjustments to arrive at equal and correct balances, and conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts to adjust or correct any difference immediately.

We recommended and Management, through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to subject all depreciable PPE accounts to depreciation and consider availing of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended and Management, through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to review all the entries affecting the above-mentioned accounts and make appropriate adjustments to reflect their correct balances.

We recommended and Management, through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to transfer the unexpended/ unobligated balance of the CY 2023 LDRRM-MOOE and QRF to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts in accordance with IPSAS 1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that Management submit proof that remedial actions were taken and that the appropriations for Waterworks were approved to legalize its utilization.

We recommended that Management secure authorization from the Sangguniang Bayan for the above-mentioned projects implemented under lump-sum appropriation and submit to the Audit Team for verification or a written explanation why the same could not be submitted.

We recommended and Management agreed that the:
Municipal Budget Officer secure computation of PS Limitation for 2023 from the Cebu Provincial Budget Office and submit to the Audit Team as proof that the 2023 PS Limitation was not exceeded despite properly charging the LCA amounting to ₱575,250.00 to Personal Services, and
Municipal Budget Officer to observe proper charging of expenses to appropriate items in the Annual Budget and the Municipal Accountant to observe proper recording of transactions in accordance with its nature and classification

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairperson of the Municipal Development Council (MDC), prioritize the preparation of the CDP 2023-2028, duly harmonized with the approved RDP and PDP 2023-2028, respectively, and its approval thereafter for the effective and efficient implementation of the priority development projects of the LGU.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct all concerned LGU officials and employees to impose warranty security in the procurement of goods in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9184, otherwise, any expenses due to the defects within the warranty period shall be charged to the concerned approving official

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the Municipal Engineer submit the Quarterly Report on PPAs with complete information on or before the 10th day of the month following the end of each quarter, otherwise, impose administrative disciplinary action to the responsible officials every time the reports are not submitted within the required time.
We recommended and Management, through the BAC Secretariat, agreed to comply with GPPB Circular No. 02-2020 and furnish the Audit Team with proof of submission of the prescribed reports to the GPPB.

We recommended that Management ensure that the:
Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording the purchase of inventories as outright expenses and use the inventory method of accounting as prescribed in the Manual of the NGAS and IPSAS 12;
Municipal Accountant reconcile the affected accounts and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances reflected in the financial statements; and
Municipal Accountant and the GSO Head prepare supply ledger cards and stock cards, respectively, and to reconcile both records regularly.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor:
1. Instruct the Inventory Committee to:
a. Prepare a Physical Inventory Plan containing the specific
assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a
schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking
activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical
inventory, among others.
b. Conduct the physical count and implement the one-time cleansing of
PPE pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
c. Cause the preparation and submission of a complete Report on the
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Audit Team
not later than January 31 of each year.
2. Require the Property Custodian to prepare and maintain property cards
and reconcile with the PPE Ledger Cards periodically; and
3. Require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Exert earnest effort in the preparation and maintenance of PPE
ledger cards;
b. Review and reconcile the PPE accounts presented in the books and
lapsing schedules, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to
reflect the correct PPE balances in the financial statements;
c. Update and reconcile PPE records and provide depreciation on
identified assets by designating the corresponding accounting
personnel who will be responsible in undertaking the same.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:
1. Municipal Accountant:
a. Exert utmost effort to retrieve/request supporting documents relative
to the book reconciling items and untraced prior years’ adjustments
aggregating P2,707,890.90 and reconcile the difference in balances
between the bank confirmation replies and the amount recorded in
the books by preparing bank reconciliation statements duly supported
with complete bank statements and record the book reconciling items,
if warranted;
b. Coordinate with the LBP-Sogod and send confirmation letter to
ensure the existence/status/balance of KALAHI account which was
not confirmed by the bank;
c. Record the unrecorded deposit in the General Fund Account
(previous account) and PHIC account amounting to P448,507.71 and
P19,300.00, respectively, as confirmed by the bank; and
2. Municipal Treasurer to process immediately the reactivation of the
dormant accounts or cause closure of the same, if not reactivated and
transfer the balance to an active account in order to protect the amount
from loss of rights and ownership.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:
1. Municipal Treasurer to return to the Bureau of Treasury the unutilized
fund transfer amounting to P2,413,077.00 as required by the Department
of Budget and Management Local Budget Circular Nos. 140 and 141 both
dated December 28, 2021;
2. Municipal Accountant to:
a. Exert utmost effort to verify the accounts with abnormal debit and
unreturned balances and send confirmation letters once the NGA will
be identified to determine if the LGU have unsettled accounts;
b. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of
the existence of the fund transfer; and
3. Municipal Agriculture Officer to implement the project immediately for
the benefit of the intended beneficiaries of the project and avoiding the
funds to be idle.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all her collections daily
or not later than the next banking day;
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare a reconciliation of the
amount of weekly/monthly collection vis-à-vis the amount of
weekly/monthly deposit as recorded in the Cashbook by submitting the
breakdown of the collections represented by each deposit slip; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Revenue Clerks that the official
receipts be written in hard indelible ballpen and the duplicate and
triplicate copies will be clear carbon reproductions in all respects of
whatever may have been written on the original.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to make representation with the
Landbank of the Philippines to discuss the termination and/or any other
possible courses of action relative to the loan agreement considering that loan
proceeds can no longer be exclusively used to finance the construction of
seawall as it was no longer continued by the contractor and was already
damaged by Typhoon Paeng.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Health
Officer, to:
1. Issue a Special Order covering the assignment of public health workers to
hazardous/difficult areas;
2. Secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOST/DOH/DND/Director of
the PHILVOCS that the place of assignment/travel is a strife-
torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or
areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano
activity or eruption; and
3. Attach the complete supporting documents to the disbursement vouchers.

We likewise recommended the Municipal Accountant process disbursement
vouchers only when they are supported with complete documentation, and
adhere strictly to the guidelines on the grant of hazard pay, subsistence, and
laundry allowance.

We recommended that the nine Public Health Workers presented in Table 19
refund of the overpaid benefits.

This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance and
Suspension.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Supply
Officer to:
a. Use the required format of a sequentially numbered Driver’s trip ticket
duly approved by the Agency Head or the authorized representative with
complete information being filled out in every detail needed for all travels
undertaken;
b. Reconcile every month the Driver’s Trip Ticket with the Monthly Fuel
Consumption and Summary of Monthly Report of Official Travels as
mandated under COA Circular No. 77-61 to determine whether the said
fuel usage is valid and is being used appropriately;
c. Utilize the prescribed form such as the Requisition and Issue Voucher,
Summary of Requisition and Issue Voucher and Daily Gas Issue Record as a
tool in the monitoring of the receipt and issuance of the fuel;
d. Enforce the “No Driver’s Trip Ticket, No Trip Policy”;
e. As to the distance travelled, to determine the accurate consumption of
fuel:
- All vehicles with functional odometer should record the kilometer reading
before and after the trip
- All vehicles with non-functional/defective odometer should indicate the
approximate distance traveled including the number of trips undertaken
during the day. Immediate repair of the odometer should be considered.
f. Submit to the Audit Team the list of vehicles which consumed the
1,565.56 liters of fuel, oil and lubricants. This is without prejudice to the
issuance of Notice of Disallowance as a result of our audit.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the:
a. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to evaluate and
ensure that all proposed PPAs in the Peace and Order and Public Safety
(POPS) Plan of the LGU are directly related to crime and disorder, illegal
drugs, conflict, threats to the environment and human security, and other
related-peace and order concerns that may be deemed necessary; and
b. Municipal Accountant to recognize Accounts Payable that are
consummated transactions with complete documentation to prove that
these are valid liabilities.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the Local
Peace and Order Council:
a. Include only those PPAs that are directly related to crime and disorder,
illegal drugs, conflict, threats to the environment and human security,
and other related-peace and order concerns that may be deemed
necessary; and
b. Refrain from using appropriation for Peace and Order for purposes other than
those authorized under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2022-118.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
1. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all necessary
documentation is prepared and maintained to demonstrate compliance
with COA regulations regarding the transfer of LDRRMF.
2. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to transfer immediately the cash
corresponding to the unexpended balances of LDRRMF for CYs 2016,
2019, 2022, and 2023 from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund
bank account.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MPDC to:
a. Address the issues affecting the delay on the bidding-related activities
and ensure that all approved PPAs are procurement and
implementation-ready and warrant the implementation of all approved
PPAs for the optimal utilization of the appropriation for development
projects; and
b. Continuously adhere to the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 to ensure that
maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and
environmental management outcome of the Municipality is achieved and
for the realization of the intended purpose of the 20% Development
Fund.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the MDRRM Officer,
together with the MDRRM Council to:
a. Timely implement all LDRRM related PPAs to achieve the goals and
objective of the fund as well as the desired benefits; and
b. Stop the use of LDRRMF for non-disaster related activities and strictly
follow the guidelines in the utilization of the LDRRMF as provided under
Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC Chairperson to
submit justification why the contractor was awarded with new contracts despite
incurrence of negative slippage and the non-disclosure of the actual accomplishment
vis-à-vis planned accomplishment of its on-going projects.

1.21. We recommended and Management agreed to completely adhere with the
guidelines provided in COA Circular 2015-008. Further, advise the
accounting unit to make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
balance of the Road Network account taking into consideration the
abovementioned deficiencies, and depreciate separately each
component of the road networks except the road lots.

1.22. Moreover, the Engineering Unit regularly inform the accountant whether
or not a particular infrastructure constitutes road networks, and if any
concrete pavement sections of roads are removed to enable
derecognition from the records of the accounting. Also, provide the latter
and the GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects.

1.23. Finally, conduct regular assessment of all local roads for any indication
of impairment and report the same to the accounting to effect the
necessary adjustment for impairment losses.

2.8. We recommended and management agreed to secure the deed
documents as well as the property acknowledgement receipts, delivery
receipts, etc. and furnish the accounting office to enable the recognition
thereof in the books of accounts. Further, ask the assistance of the
assessor in determining the fair market value of these equipment at the
time of acquisition to be used as the basis in recording and the
computation of the accumulated depreciation.

3.6. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant-designate agreed to
prepare the appropriate journal entries to recognize the Office Equipment
and CIP-Infrastructure Assets in the amount of ₱517,777.77 and
₱1,118,632.59, respectively.

4.11. We recommended and the engineering and other concerned offices
agreed to provide the accounting unit with the Certificate of Completion
and/or Turnover to enable the reclassification of the CIPs to the
appropriate asset account as well as the recognition of the accumulated
depreciation appertaining thereto.

1.8 We recommended that Municipal Mayor: (i) consider on creating
the General Services Office (GSO) which functions, among others,
focused on the management of property and supply management
of the LGU as provided in Item (t)(2)(x) of Rule XVIII of the Rules
and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of
1991; (ii) re-evaluate the composition of the Inventory Committee
ensuring that those designated as part of such Committee does
not handle significant responsibilities from their main functions or
positions; and avail the one-time cleansing of PPEs as prescribed
in COA Circular 2020-006.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee secure
complete PPE records from the Accounting Office and use the
same as guide in the conduct of their actual physical count.

2.7 We recommended that the accountant conduct an in-depth
investigation to find the root cause of abnormal/negative balances
and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the
negative/abnormal balances to their proper account. Also,
establish an internal control that would help prevent, detect, and
correct issues as they occur.

3.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the
following adjusting entries:
Trust Liabilities ₱5,176,747.50
Prior Period Adjustments ₱5,176,747.50
Henceforth, ensure that all prior period adjustments due to effect
of changes in accounting policy and adjustments due to errors be
recorded under the Prior Period Adjustment account.

4.7 We recommended that (i) The Municipal Treasurer in coordination
with the LDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant, immediately
transfer the unspent current year QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the
DRRM Special Trust Fund, pursuant to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular
No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; (ii) The Municipal
Accountant record the transfer by effecting the following adjusting
entry:
In the General Fund books:
Prior Period Adjustments ₱4,078,807.89
Cash ₱4,078,807.89
In the Trust Fund books:
Cash ₱4,078,807.89
Trust Liabilities-DRRM ₱4,078,807.89
Henceforth, ensure the timely transfer of the unspent balance of
LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that Management utilize physical data storage devices as backup repositories for all its files in case of file deletion or destruction. 

We further recommended that Management enroll its deposit accounts in online banking to obtain access to information needed that can facilitate in the preparation of the monthly BRS even without the physical copies of its bank statements.  

Finally, we recommended that Management perform and submit the monthly BRS in accordance with COA Circular 96 – 011.

We recommended that Management require the contractor to post the required security under Item 4.2 of Annex “E” of the RIRR R.A. 9184 since the advance payment has not yet been fully repaid to reduce the Municipality’s exposure to risk of loss arising from non-performance of the contractor’s obligation.

Henceforth, we recommended that Management review the provisions of Annex “E” of the RIRR of R.A. 9184 to ensure that succeeding projects will be covered by all the securities required under the law to adequately protect the Municipality in case of breach by the contractor.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit the approved contracts and individual accomplishment reports on the said payroll.  

Henceforth, strictly observe compliance with the documentary requirements prescribed under COA Circular 2012-001 on the payment of Salaries and Wages of JO Workers.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly adhere to the provisions of the RIRR R.A. 9184 to protect the Municipality from possible losses due to unfaithful performance of the winning bidder of its obligation under the contract.
We recommended and Management agreed to strictly observe the required allocation of at least 5 per cent of its Annual Budget to strengthen its GAD programs, projects, and activities.

We recommended that Management send demand letters to the ineligible beneficiaries for the refund of the illegally received assistance totaling ₱1,140,000.00 and ensure that these illegally disbursed funds are collected and returned to DSWD.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to: 1. Instruct the Inventory Committee to: a. Prepare a Physical Inventory Plan containing the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory, among others. b. Conduct the physical count and implement the one-time cleansing of PPE pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006; and c. Require the Property Custodian to prepare and maintain property cards and reconcile with the PPE Ledger Cards periodically; and 4. Require the Municipal Accountant to exert earnest effort in the preparation and maintenance of PPE ledger cards, update and reconciliation of PPE records and provision of depreciation on identified assets by designating the corresponding accounting personnel who will be responsible in undertaking the same.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct:
a. The Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with the certified list of taxpayers showing the amount of tax due and collectible, to serve as basis for the set-up of the correct RPT/SET Receivables in the books, due pursuant to Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
b. The Municipal Treasurer together with the Municipal Accountant to establish RPT Receivable/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/Deferred SET with the corresponding SLs for each debtor based on the duly Certified List to avoid misleading information in the Annual Financial Report.
c. The MTO and MAO undertake periodic reconciliation of RPT/SET receivables with the reported delinquent taxes and make necessary adjustments to avoid misstatement of RPT/SET Receivables and serve as basis for the subsequent collection of taxes.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer together with the Municipal Accountant had already complied with the recommendation.
Established RPT Receivable/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/Deferred SET with the corresponding SLs for each debtor based on the Certified List to avoid misleading information in the Annual Financial Report.
Periodic reconciliation of the RPT/SET receivables were already undertaken.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant.
